title: The Periodic Table of Operators
subtitle: Organizing semantic primitives by domain and transformation type — and predicting what's missing

children.children: What if semantic primitives could be organized like chemical elements — by domain and transformation type — so that gaps in the table predict undiscovered operators? This tutorial explores the Periodic Table of Operators: a systematic classification that connects to Wierzbicka's Natural Semantic Metalanguage and cross-linguistic universals.

In 1869, Mendeleev organized chemical elements by atomic weight and properties, creating the periodic table. The genius wasn't just classification — it was that gaps in the table predicted undiscovered elements. He predicted gallium, scandium, and germanium before they were found.

Can we do the same for meaning? If we organize Schankian operators along two axes — the domain they operate in and the type of transformation they perform — do patterns emerge? Do gaps predict missing primitives?
Axis 1: Domain (What kind of thing changes?)
Events happen in different domains of reality. A physical movement is fundamentally different from a mental shift or a social exchange. We identify four primary domains:
🏗️ Physical — Objects move, break, combine, change location or form
🧠 Mental — Thoughts form, beliefs shift, knowledge transfers
🤝 Social — Ownership changes, obligations form, relationships shift
❤️ Emotional — Feelings arise, moods shift, desires form or dissolve
Axis 2: Transformation Type (What kind of change?)
Independently of domain, transformations fall into a small set of types. Things can be transferred, created, destroyed, modified, or perceived:
↔️ Transfer — Something moves from A to B (location, ownership, information)
✨ Creation — Something new comes into existence
💥 Destruction — Something ceases to exist or function
🔧 Modification — Something changes properties but persists
👁️ Perception — An agent becomes aware of something (no change to the thing itself)
Each cell in this 4×5 grid is a potential operator. Some are well-known Schankian primitives. Others are gaps — predictions of operators that should exist.

props.title: 🧪 The Periodic Table of Semantic Operators

children.children: Click any cell to explore it. Notice: Schank's original primitives cluster heavily in the Physical and Mental-Transfer cells. The Social and Emotional domains are mostly gaps — predicted operators that should exist but weren't formalized.
The most exciting part of a periodic table isn't what's filled in — it's what's missing. Each gap is a hypothesis: a semantic transformation that should be expressible as a primitive operator.

props.title: Why are Emotional operators missing from Schank?

children.children: Schank's Conceptual Dependency theory (1972) was designed for story understanding — tracking physical objects, information flow, and social transactions. Emotions were treated as side-effects rather than first-class transformations.
But emotions DO transform states: anger changes decision-making, empathy transfers emotional states between agents, grief destroys motivation. A complete operator set needs emotional primitives.
Wierzbicka's Natural Semantic Metalanguage (NSM) includes FEEL and WANT as semantic primes — evidence that emotional operators are linguistically universal.

BOND? (Social-Creation): When two agents form a new relationship — friendship, alliance, marriage. The social graph gains an edge. No existing Schankian primitive captures this; ATRANS handles transfer of existing things, not creation of new connections.

SHIFT? (Emotional-Modification): When an emotion transforms into another — anger softening into pity, excitement curdling into anxiety. The emotion persists but changes character. Distinct from creation (AROUSE) or destruction (CALM).

FORGET? (Mental-Destruction): The inverse of MBUILD. Knowledge or belief is lost or becomes inaccessible. Schank had no primitive for this — a striking gap given how central forgetting is to narrative (secrets, amnesia, deception).
If the periodic table captures real structure, its operators should appear across all human languages. Two independent research programs provide evidence:

Wierzbicka's NSM identifies 64 semantic primes — concepts expressible in every known language. Many map directly to our table cells: MOVE (Physical-Transfer), KNOW (Mental-Perception), WANT (Emotional-Creation of desire), DO (Physical-Modification).

A 2016 PNAS study analyzed 81 languages and found that certain semantic domains (body, kinship, spatial relations) have universal structure — the same conceptual neighborhoods appear regardless of language family. Our table's domain axis aligns with these cross-linguistic clusters.

props.title: NSM's 64 primes vs. our table's ~20 cells — what's the relationship?

children.children: NSM primes include more than just operators — they include substantives (I, YOU, SOMETHING), determiners (THIS, THE SAME), and evaluators (GOOD, BAD). If we filter to just the action/transformation primes, we get roughly: DO, HAPPEN, MOVE, SAY, THINK, KNOW, WANT, FEEL, SEE, HEAR, LIVE, DIE, MAKE, TOUCH.

That's 14 primes — strikingly close to Schank's original ~14 primitives, and they map well onto our table. This convergence from two completely independent methodologies (Schank's AI-driven analysis vs. Wierzbicka's cross-linguistic fieldwork) is strong evidence that the table captures real structure.

props.title: 6. Using the Table for Operator Discovery

children.children: The periodic table isn't just taxonomy — it's a tool for discovering new operators. Here's the methodology:

props.title: 🔍 Gap-Driven Discovery: Fill in a predicted operator

children.children: If we train a neural system to discover operators from data (as proposed throughout our research), the periodic table provides concrete predictions:

Prediction: A well-trained operator discovery system should find roughly 4 × 5 = 20 operator clusters, organized along domain and transformation-type axes. If it finds significantly fewer, it's under-differentiating. If significantly more, it may be overfitting to surface forms.
1. Train operator embeddings on a large event corpus
2. Apply PCA or UMAP to the learned operator space
3. Check: Do operators cluster into domain groups along one axis?
4. Check: Do transformation types form a second orthogonal axis?
5. Check: Are the 'gap' regions populated by learned operators that Schank missed?
If all three checks pass, the periodic table is validated as a structural prediction about semantic space.

questions.question: What makes the periodic table analogy powerful for operator discovery?
options: It provides a catchy name for the framework
Gaps in the table predict undiscovered operators
It proves operators are chemical in nature
It reduces the number of operators needed
questions.explanation: Like Mendeleev predicting gallium from gaps in his table, empty cells in our domain × transformation grid predict semantic operators that should exist but haven't been formalized.

questions.question: Which domain is LEAST covered by Schank's original primitives?
questions.explanation: Schank's CD theory focused on physical actions and information flow. Emotional transformations were treated as side-effects, leaving the entire Emotional row as predicted gaps.

questions.question: How does Wierzbicka's NSM relate to the periodic table?
options: NSM disproves the need for operators
NSM's ~14 action primes converge with our ~20 cells from a completely independent methodology
NSM proves exactly 64 operators are needed
NSM only applies to Indo-European languages
questions.explanation: NSM's cross-linguistic fieldwork identified universal semantic primes independently of Schank's AI research. The convergence of both approaches on ~14-20 fundamental action/transformation concepts strongly validates the periodic table structure.

questions.question: A trained operator discovery system finds only 8 clusters. What does the table predict?
options: The system is optimal — 8 is the true number
The system is under-differentiating — it's collapsing distinct transformation types
questions.explanation: The table predicts ~20 operators (4 domains × 5 types). Finding only 8 suggests the system is merging operators that should be distinct — perhaps collapsing all emotional transformations into one cluster, or failing to distinguish creation from modification.

children.children: This periodic table framework connects to several other brainstorm entries: Entry #45 (MDL) provides the compression test for whether the table has the right number of cells. Entry #12 (Disentangled Dimensions) suggests domain and transformation type should be independent learned dimensions. Entry #47 (Renormalization Group) views operators as fixed points — the table predicts which fixed points should exist. Entry #39 (Information Bottleneck) discovers primitives via compression — the table predicts what compression should find.
